Percent followers represents the freedom to maneuver and the comfort and convenience of travel. It can also serve as a proxy for the need to provide passing opportunities.
Follower density is defined as the number of vehicles in a follower state per mile per lane. Mathematically, it is the percent followers multiplied by density.
The general relationship between percent followers and flow rate is non-linear...
Follower density is used as the service measure for two-lane highways because of its sensitivity to both traffic demand and geometric alignment variability.
The general relationship between follower density and flow rate is illustrated in Exhibit 15-4.

*Estimating FFS* The posted speed limit serves as a surrogate for identifying FFS for base geometric conditions (i.e., lane width ≥ 12 ft, shoulder width ≥ 6 ft, no access points) and no heavy vehicles in the traffic stream.

As the amount of platooning increases, the highway’s LOS deteriorates.

Exhibit 15-A1 presents results from simulation that show values of the reduction in percent followers in level terrain for different combinations of passing lane lengths and directional traffic flow rates
Providing a passing lane on a two-lane highway can improve operational performance and LOS, as it provides passing opportunities that help break up vehicular platoons.
The truck speed–distance curves in Exhibit 15-A3 through Exhibit 15-A5 can be used to determine speed reductions on different lengths of grade for three truck types
The upgrade speed versus distance functions were developed assuming an initial speed of 75 mi/h. These functions can be modified to produce upgrade speeds for different initial speeds.

Had the segment length not been adjusted, the final speed would have equaled 50 mi/h. Therefore, the 15 mi/h difference in initial speed created a 10 mi/h difference in the final speed on the upgrade.

Exhibit 15-A9 through Exhibit 15-A11 provide additional upgrade segment lengths for various combinations of initial speed and grade slope, for single-unit, intermediate semitrailer, and interstate semitrailer trucks, respectively.
Exhibit 15-A12 provides the segment lengths at which crawl speed is reached if the truck enters the segment with an initial speed of 75 mi/h.
